did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper explicitly uses a Back Propagation Neural Network (BPN) for the classification of malignant melanoma and benign skin lesions from dermoscopy images. It also employs feature extraction techniques such as Local Binary Patterns (LBP), Gray-Level Co-occurrence Matrix (GLCM), and the ABCD rule to feed into the neural network model.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents an original experimental study proposing a specific classification system using a back propagation neural network and the ABCD rule for malignant melanoma and benign skin lesions. It details a 'Proposed system' with sections on preprocessing, feature extraction (LBP, GLCM), and neural network implementation, followed by a 'Results' section reporting performance metrics (accuracy 95%, sensitivity 90%) on a dataset. This structure and content indicate it is an original technical study rather than a review.

title: Classification of malignant melanoma and benign skin lesion by using back propagation neural network and ABCD rule
brief_summary: This study proposes a computer-aided diagnostic system for classifying malignant melanoma and benign skin lesions using dermoscopy images. The methodology employs the ABCD rule (Asymmetry, Border, Color, Diameter) alongside texture feature extraction techniques, specifically Local Binary Patterns (LBP) and Gray-Level Co-occurrence Matrix (GLCM). These features are processed through a Back Propagation Neural Network (BPN) to determine the malignancy stage. The system was evaluated using standard performance metrics including accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity. The proposed method achieved an accuracy of 95% and a sensitivity of 90%, demonstrating its effectiveness in distinguishing between malignant and benign lesions.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target class for classification. The paper focuses on recognizing malignant melanoma using the ABCD rule and neural networks. It is described as a 'dangerous disorder' and 'most generally recognized one' among human growths in the context of skin cancer.

The comparator class to malignant melanoma. The study aims to classify both malignant melanoma and benign skin lesions. The title explicitly mentions 'benign skin lesion' and the abstract mentions 'benign melanocytic lesions' in the context of classification.

all_task_types.target: Malignant melanoma vs. benign skin lesions
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly focuses on the classification of malignant melanoma and benign skin lesions using a Back Propagation Neural Network (BPN). The title is 'Classification of malignant melanoma and benign skin lesion...'. The abstract states the goal is to 'recognize skin growth' and 'determine the malignancy stage' (binary classification of dangerous vs. benign). The results report accuracy (95%) and sensitivity (90%), which are standard metrics for classification tasks. Although the abstract mentions 'multi classification results' in the context of neural network capabilities, the specific application described is distinguishing between malignant and benign lesions.

datasets_used.name: Custom Dermoscopy Image Dataset
datasets_used.description: A proprietary dataset of dermoscopy images used for the proposed classification system. The paper does not specify the source (e.g., public repository or hospital collection) or the specific collection method, referring to it only as 'input' and 'database loading'.

use.training: Used to train the Back Propagation Neural Network (BPN) and extract features (ABCD rule, LBP, GLCM).
use.testing: Used to evaluate the system's performance, reporting accuracy of 95% and sensitivity of 90%.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. It reported performance metrics such as accuracy (95%) and sensitivity (90%) without statistical inference.

Custom Dermoscopy Image Dataset.notes: The paper reports an accuracy of 95% and a sensitivity of 90% for the proposed method. Specificity is mentioned as a metric used for evaluation but no numerical value is provided in the text. The system utilizes a Back Propagation Neural Network (BPN) with 20 neurons, incorporating features from the ABCD rule (Asymmetry, Border, Color, Diameter), Local Binary Patterns (LBP), and Gray-Level Co-occurrence Matrix (GLCM).

architecture_details.layers: Input layer, hidden layer(s), output layer (specific layer counts not reported)

classification_or_output_head.classes: Malignant melanoma vs. Benign skin lesions
classification_or_output_head.details: Classifies based on features extracted from ABCD rule, LBP, and GLCM

preprocessing_techniques: Image enhancement (contrast and edge enhancement, pseudo-shading, amplifying, honing)
RGB to HSV color space conversion
conclusions: The proposed system, which combines the ABCD rule with texture feature extraction (LBP and GLCM) and a Back Propagation Neural Network (BPN), achieved an accuracy of 95% and a sensitivity of 90% in classifying malignant melanoma and benign skin lesions.
The study demonstrates that using the ABCD rule (Asymmetry, Border, Color, Diameter) alongside texture features is effective for the early recognition of malignant melanoma.

The authors acknowledge that while the proposed method is effective, many other plans exist to distinguish melanocytic injuries, and the choice of parameters is based on accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity.

The system utilizes dermoscopy images and processes them through preprocessing, segmentation, and feature extraction (including symmetry, border, color, and diameter) to determine the malignancy stage.
important_remarks: The study uses a custom, proprietary dataset of dermoscopy images without specifying the source, size, or collection method, raising concerns about reproducibility and potential selection bias.

The paper does not report the number of images used for training, validation, or testing, nor does it provide a breakdown of class distribution (malignant vs. benign), making it impossible to assess class imbalance or overfitting risks.
No external validation was performed; the model was only evaluated on the internal dataset, limiting the generalizability of the reported 95% accuracy and 90% sensitivity.
The paper does not report specificity values despite listing it as a metric, nor does it provide confidence intervals, p-values, or statistical significance tests for the results.

The neural network architecture is described as having '20 neurons' but lacks details on the number of layers, activation functions, weight initialization, or hyperparameters (learning rate, epochs), hindering replication.

The authors acknowledge that 'many plans are there to distinguish the melanocytic injury,' suggesting the proposed method is one of many competing approaches without comparative benchmarking against state-of-the-art methods.

The preprocessing and feature extraction steps (ABCD rule, LBP, GLCM) rely on manual or semi-automated definitions of symmetry, border, color, and diameter, which may introduce subjectivity or inconsistency compared to fully automated deep learning approaches.

The paper mentions 'multi classification results' in the abstract but focuses on binary classification (malignant vs. benign) in the results, creating ambiguity about the scope of the model's capabilities.
